title: Simple Sourdough Recipe for a Wedding Party
keyword: simple sourdough recipe wedding party wooden spurtle
meta_description: Discover a simple sourdough recipe perfect for a wedding party, plus pro tips on using a wooden spurtle to mix and scrape your starter flawlessly.
html:
When you’re planning a wedding party menu, a warm, crusty loaf of sourdough can be the show‑stopping side that feels both rustic and elegant. This simple sourdough recipe wedding party wooden spurtle guide walks you through every step—from feeding the starter to scoring the loaf—while showing why the 2 PCS Wooden Spurtle Spatula for Sourdough Starter is the perfect tool for mixing, jar‑scraping, and handling delicate dough.

Why Sourdough Works for a Wedding Party

Sourdough brings a natural tang, airy crumb, and chewy crust that pairs beautifully with everything from charcuterie boards to hearty soups. Because it relies on a living starter rather than commercial yeast, you can make the dough ahead of time and bake fresh on the day of the event. The slow fermentation also develops flavor, so guests get a loaf that tastes far more complex than a standard bakery baguette.

Gathering Your Ingredients and Tools

Everything you need is pantry‑friendly, but the right tools make the process smoother. Aside from a large mixing bowl, a kitchen scale, and a bench scraper, the star of the show is a wooden spurtle. The 2 PCS Wooden Spurtle Spatula for Sourdough Starter (also called a Danish dough whisk) is thin enough to stir the starter without introducing air bubbles, and its smooth wood surface won’t scratch glass jars when you scrape the last few drops of starter.

  • 500 g bread flour (plus extra for dusting)
  • 350 g water, lukewarm
  • 100 g active sourdough starter
  • 10 g sea salt
  • Optional: herbs, olives, or roasted garlic for added flavor

Having a wooden spurtle on hand means you can stir the starter and later the dough without worrying about metal reacting with the acidity of the starter.

Step‑by‑Step: Simple Sourdough Recipe for a Wedding Party

1. Feed the starter. The night before the event, feed 100 g of your starter with equal parts flour and water. Use the wooden spurtle to blend the mixture until smooth; the thin tip reaches the bottom of the jar, ensuring every bit of flour is incorporated.

2. Autolyse. In a large bowl combine 500 g flour and 350 g water. Mix with the wooden spurtle until no dry spots remain. Let sit 30 minutes. This rest period hydrates the flour and improves gluten development.

3. Add starter and salt. Sprinkle the fed starter and 10 g salt over the autolysed dough. Using the spurtle, fold the starter into the dough, then knead briefly by hand until the mixture feels cohesive.

4. Bulk fermentation. Cover the bowl and let the dough rise at room temperature (around 75°F) for 4–5 hours. Every 30 minutes for the first two hours, perform a set of stretch‑and‑folds: pull a corner of the dough up, then fold it over itself. The wooden spurtle is handy for loosening sticky dough between folds.

5. Shape the loaf. Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface. Shape into a tight boule or bâtard, depending on your presentation. Use the spurtle’s tapered end to smooth the seam and create a subtle ridge that will help the loaf expand evenly in the oven.

6. Proof. Place the shaped loaf into a proofing basket or a bowl lined with a floured kitchen towel. Let it proof for 2 hours, or refrigerate overnight for a deeper flavor. The next morning, preheat your oven to 475°F with a Dutch oven inside.

7. Bake. Transfer the loaf into the hot Dutch oven, score the top with a razor blade, and cover. Bake 20 minutes, then remove the lid and bake another 20–25 minutes until the crust is deep golden brown. Let the bread cool on a wire rack for at least 30 minutes before slicing.

Using the Wooden Spurtle for Perfect Results

The wooden spurtle isn’t just a novelty; its design solves two common sourdough headaches. First, the narrow, tapered shape scrapes every last drop of starter from the jar, preserving the culture and preventing waste. Second, the smooth wood surface is gentle on glass and ceramic bowls, reducing the risk of micro‑scratches that can harbor bacteria. When stirring a thick starter or a sticky dough, the spurtle moves through the mixture without bending or breaking, giving you consistent control.

After use, rinse the spurtle under warm water, dry it promptly, and rub a light coat of food‑grade mineral oil once a month. This maintenance keeps the wood from absorbing moisture and extends the life of your Danish‑style dough whisk.

Serving Ideas for a Wedding Party

Once your sourdough is baked and cooled, slice it into generous pieces. Pair it with a simple herb‑butter, an assortment of artisanal cheeses, or a drizzle of olive oil and sea salt. For a more festive presentation, serve the loaf on a wooden board surrounded by fresh grapes, figs, and a few small bowls of tapenade. The aroma of freshly baked sourdough will fill the room, creating a warm, welcoming atmosphere for guests as they mingle.

FAQ

Can I use a metal whisk instead of a wooden spurtle?

Yes, but a metal whisk can introduce tiny air bubbles into the starter and may scratch glass jars. The wooden spurtle’s smooth surface eliminates both concerns, making it the ideal tool for delicate starter work.

How far in advance can I bake the sourdough before the wedding?

Cool the loaf completely, then wrap it tightly in a clean tea towel and store it in a bread box for up to 24 hours. Re‑heat in a 350°F oven for 10 minutes to revive the crust.

Is the wooden spurtle safe for dishwasher use?

We recommend hand‑washing only. The heat and moisture of a dishwasher can cause the wood to warp or split over time.
